title: Cabinet Console Table
summary: A 72"-wide console with a real face frame and four inset cabinet doors — a proper cabinet build, not a shelf-and-legs console.
description:
Unlike an open-shelf console, this one is a genuine face-frame cabinet: a plywood-backed 1x12 carcass with a center divider, faced with a mitered 1x3 frame, and closed up with four inset doors. Inset doors are the detail that separates a nice piece from an average one, and they're also the detail most likely to go wrong — get the door sizing and gap consistent and it reads as furniture; get it inconsistent and every uneven gap draws the eye.

Build and attach the face frame before the back goes on, not after — you need to be able to get a drill inside to drive the pocket screws, and a plywood back closes that access off completely. Size each door 1/4" less than its opening in both width and height, which nets an even 1/8" gap around all four sides once it's hung — measure your actual openings before cutting the doors, since real-world lumber width rarely matches a plan exactly.

tools.note: For the doors and any rip cuts.

materials.name: Pine, 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: Top, sides, center divider, bottom shelf, shelves.

cutList.note: Cut to fit each side of the base.
Size to your actual openings — 1/4" less in width and height for an even 1/8" gap all around.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut one top at 72", two sides at 35-1/4", one center divider at 31-1/2", one bottom shelf at 69-1/2" and two interior shelves at 34-3/8" from the Pine 1x12. Cut one back panel at 71" x 36" from the 1/4" plywood. Cut one top front trim at 72" from the Pine 1x2. From the Pine 1x3, cut two face frame sides at 32-3/4", one face frame header at 71", one face frame footer at 66" — and cut the center divider trim at 30-1/4", NOT the 27-1/4" the cut list shows, because it has to span the same height as the openings it divides (32-3/4" less the 2-1/2" footer). Leave the base moulding and all four doors uncut for now; both get measured off the real cabinet later. Allowing about 1/8" per saw cut, the 1x12 parts need FIVE 8 ft boards, not the four the materials list declares — no two of them pair on a 96" board, since the shortest is 31-1/2" and the longest offcut is 26-1/2". Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the carcass
steps.body: Assemble the two 35-1/4" sides, the 69-1/2" bottom shelf, the 31-1/2" center divider and the 72" top into a box. The bottom shelf runs between the sides, so the carcass finishes 71" wide — which is why the face frame header and the back panel are both 71", and why the 72" top overhangs just 1/2" at each end. Drill the pocket holes for the face frame NOW, before assembly, with the 3/4" setting and the holes facing forward: once the face frame is on and the back is closed you cannot get a drill in there. Glue and screw with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, and check the diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the frame flat on the bench before it goes anywhere near the cabinet: the two 32-3/4" sides stand full height, the 71" header runs across the top of them, and the 66" footer fits between them at the bottom — 71" less two 2-1/2" sides is exactly 66". Then set the 30-1/4" center divider trim between the header and footer at the midpoint, which leaves two openings of 31-3/4" wide by 30-1/4" tall. Join it all with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, check it for square across the diagonals, and only then glue and pin it to the front of the carcass with 1-1/4" brad nails. The frame has to go on before the back does, so you still have drill access inside.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Nail the plywood back panel on with 3/4" brad nails, checking the carcass for square right before it goes on — the back is your last chance to correct it.

steps.title: Add interior shelving
steps.body: The two 34-3/8" interior shelves are cut to exactly half the interior: 69-1/2" between the sides, less the 3/4" center divider, halved. Set them on shelf pins if you want them adjustable, or fix them with pocket holes drilled on their undersides where nothing will see them. Whichever you choose, hold both shelves at the same height off the bottom in each bay — with inset doors the shelf line shows through the opening every time a door is open, and a mismatch reads immediately.

steps.title: Add base trim
steps.body: Measure the finished base and cut the moulding to it rather than to the cut list's four 20" pieces — the base is 72" across the front and 11-1/4" deep on each side, which needs roughly 94-1/2" of moulding plus waste at each miter, so the single 8 ft stick declared will not go all the way around and four 20" pieces come nowhere close. Cut outside miters at the two front corners, glue and pin the front piece on first, then fit each return to it. Hold the top edge of the moulding to one consistent line off the floor; a base trim that wanders is more obvious than an uneven door gap.

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: Measure your two openings directly before cutting any door part; built to plan they are 31-3/4" wide by 30-1/4" tall, but real 1x3 width varies and the openings are what rule. Be aware that the plan is internally inconsistent here: it gives TWO openings but lists four doors, four pairs of hinges and four knobs at 15-1/2" x 15" each, which is neither a full nor a half opening with any consistent reveal — decide whether you are hanging one door per opening or a pair, and size accordingly. Whichever you choose, build each door 1/4" less than the space it fills in both width and height, which nets an even 1/8" gap on all four sides once hinged. Hang them, adjust until every gap reads the same, and add a magnetic catch if your hinges do not hold the doors shut on their own.

images.alt: A wide cabinet console table with a face frame and four inset doors
